React, Might As Well Learn It Now - StrictMode and Web Vitals in CRA (20)
React, Might As Well Learn It Now

Picking up from the last post's CRA project, today we'll finally go through the remaining 20% of the generated code we haven't looked at yet. So this one is going to be pretty light and easy. We'll cover what reportWebVitals (Web Vitals) actually does, what React.StrictMode is for, and why your useEffect sometimes runs twice.
Learn React #19 — Spinning Up a Project with Create React App (CRA)
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now?

This kicks off a brand new chapter — the Create React App (CRA) chapter. From here on we'll slowly move away from CodePen and start working in the kind of development environment you'd actually use for real projects. I'll walk through what CRA is, how to spin up a project, and what the generated folder structure looks like.
Learn React Sooner or Later, So Why Not Now? - React Advanced - Custom Hooks - (17)
Learn React Sooner or Later, So Why Not Now?

We've met a bunch of React Hooks in the earlier chapters, but every one of them was provided by React itself. So what if you want to build your own Hook? In this post we'll get to know Custom Hooks — what they actually are, the one rule you have to follow, and a couple of small examples (useKeyDown and useLog) to make it click.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - React Advanced - useRef - (16)

Next up is a pretty fun Hook called useRef. What's so special about it? In short, it doesn't trigger a re-render, it hands you back an object with a current property, and it's great for holding values that shouldn't change — or for grabbing a DOM node. Let's take a look.
React Day 14 — useCallback, or How I Stopped an Infinite setTimeout Loop

This one's a short and easy chapter. We're just going to quickly get to know useCallback. So what is useCallback? It's pretty much a variation of useMemo — but instead of memoizing a value, it memoizes a function. We'll look at a fun little example where a component falls into an infinite re-render loop, and how useCallback gets us out of it.
React Day 13: useMemo, Basically React's Version of Vue Computed

Starting this chapter we're going to meet a new Hook: useMemo. But before we get into useMemo itself, let's take a quick detour back to Vue and look at something pretty similar — Vue's Computed. Once you've seen how Computed only recalculates when its data changes, useMemo will feel a lot more familiar.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - Going Deeper Into JSX - (12)

We've already met the basics of JSX in earlier chapters, but in this post we're going to dig in deeper. The goal is to hit fewer surprises while you're building, and even pick up a few handy tricks along the way — things like JSX being syntactic sugar over createElement, self-closing tags, dot-notation namespaces, why component names must start with a capital letter, and which value types JSX quietly ignores.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? — TodoList, Part 2 (11)

Last time we built a bare-bones React TodoList. In this one we're going to polish it up — pull a chunk of the markup out into its own component, and wire up LocalStorage so your todos actually survive a page refresh. Along the way we'll run into a fun gotcha with useState being asynchronous, and figure out why useEffect is the right tool for the job (and where it isn't).
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? — React Basics: Building a TodoList (Part 1) - (10)

In this chapter we're going to actually build something small. The whole point is to pull together everything we've covered so far, because knowledge with zero output is honestly kind of useless. So let's roll up our sleeves and get some practice in. We'll build a TodoList that adds items, marks them done with a checkbox, shows a count, and clears everything at once.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - React Basics - useContext - (9)

In the earlier chapters we got a first look at how React passes data between components with Props. The thing is, Props always flow from the top down. That one-way flow keeps your data predictable, but it also creates a classic headache: you end up passing data way too deep. So in this chapter we'll meet something new that fixes exactly that — useContext.
